o: Event-driven serverless code that scales on demand
why: Azure Functions run small pieces of event-driven code serverlessly, scaling automatically.

q: How do Azure Functions compare to Azure Logic Apps?
o: Functions are code-first; Logic Apps are designer/connector-first orchestration
Functions cannot be triggered by events
why: Functions are code-centric; Logic Apps are low-code orchestrations with connectors.

q: Decide whether each Functions statement is true.

rows.s: Functions can be triggered by HTTP, timers, queues and more.
WebJobs are an older approach that Functions build upon.
Functions must always run on a dedicated VM you manage.

why: Functions support many triggers, evolved from WebJobs, and offer serverless (no VM management) plans.

q: On the Consumption plan, what happens when there is no traffic?
o: The app can scale to zero (cold starts possible)
why: Consumption scales to zero when idle, which can introduce cold starts.

q: Which plan is designed to reduce cold starts while keeping serverless scaling?
why: The Premium plan keeps pre-warmed instances to avoid cold starts while scaling elastically.

q: Which hosting option runs Functions inside a containerized environment?
why: Functions can run on Azure Container Apps for containerized hosting.

q: Decide whether each hosting/scaling statement is true.

rows.s: The Flex Consumption plan offers serverless scale with more configuration than classic Consumption.
Dedicated (App Service) plan runs on instances you provision.
Function apps have no time-out considerations at all.

why: Flex Consumption adds configurability; Dedicated uses provisioned instances; time-out duration varies by plan.

q: A long-running function keeps hitting a time-out. What likely determines that limit?
o: The hosting plan's function time-out duration
why: Function time-out duration depends on the hosting plan (e.g., stricter on Consumption).
